title: 💰 Self-Employment Tax 2026: Real Numbers Explained
description:
How self-employment tax actually works in 2026 for US freelancers, plus the UK National Insurance equivalent. Real numbers, real strategy, no fluff.

Personal finance, freelancer taxes, self employment tax 2026, NI for self employed UK.

segments.voice: Self-employment tax is the tax that punches every freelancer in the gut the first time they see it. The IRS Self-Employment Contributions Act applies a fifteen point three percent rate on net earnings, on top of regular income tax. That is twelve point four percent for Social Security and two point nine percent for Medicare. Today, what it actually means and how to plan around it.
segments.visual: person reading tax form self employment

segments.voice: Quick history. When you work for an employer in the US, your paystub shows a seven point sixty-five percent FICA deduction. The employer pays a matching seven point sixty-five percent that you never see. When you go self-employed, you become both employee and employer, so you pay both halves. That is where fifteen point three comes from.

The base for two thousand twenty-six. The Social Security wage base, set annually by the Social Security Administration, is one hundred seventy-eight thousand dollars for two thousand twenty-six. So twelve point four percent applies only on net earnings up to that ceiling. Above it, you still pay the two point nine percent Medicare portion, plus an additional zero point nine percent on earnings above two hundred thousand single, two fifty married.

Here is the calculation that actually matters. Your gross revenue minus your business expenses equals net profit on Schedule C. Multiply that by ninety-two point thirty-five percent to get net earnings from self-employment. Then apply fifteen point three percent up to the wage base. The IRS lets you deduct half of the SE tax against income tax via Schedule 1, an above-the-line deduction.

A worked example. Sarah, a freelance designer in Austin, has gross revenue of seventy thousand dollars and business expenses of fifteen thousand. Her net profit is fifty-five thousand. Times ninety-two point thirty-five percent equals fifty thousand seven hundred ninety-three. Times fifteen point three percent equals seven thousand seven hundred seventy-one in self-employment tax alone, before any income tax.

Now the income tax layer. Sarah deducts half her SE tax, three thousand eight hundred eighty-six, plus she takes the standard deduction of fifteen thousand for two thousand twenty-six. Her taxable income drops to roughly thirty-six thousand. At single filer rates, that puts her in the twelve percent bracket with a few hundred dollars at the twenty-two percent edge. Federal income tax adds about three thousand nine hundred.

Total federal tax bite for Sarah, eleven thousand six hundred dollars on fifty-five thousand of profit, roughly twenty-one percent effective. Add state tax on top, which varies. Texas has zero state income tax. California would add another three thousand or so. The Tax Foundation publishes a state-by-state effective rate map updated annually that walks through this.

The estimated payments rule. The IRS expects you to pay tax as you earn it, not in a lump sum next April. Form 1040-ES handles four quarterly payments. Miss them and the underpayment penalty hits, currently around eight percent annualized per the Federal Reserve update of October two thousand twenty-five. The dates are April fifteenth, June fifteenth, September fifteenth, and January fifteenth.

Safe harbor rule. The IRS lets you avoid the underpayment penalty if you pay either ninety percent of current year tax or one hundred percent of last year's tax, one hundred ten percent if your prior year AGI was over one hundred fifty thousand. Most freelancers use the prior-year method because it is predictable. Just take last year's tax bill, divide by four, and pay it.

Critical move, separate accounts and a tax holding bucket. Open a separate business checking and a savings account labeled tax. Every time a client invoice gets paid, transfer twenty-five to thirty percent into the tax bucket. When quarterly payments hit, the money is already there. The Federal Trade Commission has flagged commingling as one of the top three reasons small businesses lose deduction documentation in audits.
segments.visual: two bank accounts tax bucket savings

segments.voice: S corporation election, a real money saver above a threshold. If your net profit consistently runs above seventy or eighty thousand a year, electing S corp status with Form 2553 lets you split income between salary and distributions. The salary portion is subject to FICA, distributions are not. Done correctly per IRS guidance, this can save five to ten thousand dollars a year in SE tax.

The catch with S corp. The IRS requires reasonable compensation for the work the owner actually does. Pay yourself too little salary and an audit will reclassify distributions back to salary, with penalties. Reasonable compensation studies from RCReports show market rates for various professions. A solo software developer might justify sixty thousand salary on a one hundred twenty thousand profit, paying SE tax only on that sixty.
segments.visual: salary distribution split S corp diagram

segments.voice: Cost of the S corp. Setup is two to three hundred dollars in state filing fees. Annual costs include payroll service like Gusto or OnPay at around five hundred dollars a year, plus a CPA for the corporate tax return at eight hundred to fifteen hundred. So total annual overhead runs about fifteen hundred to two thousand. Below seventy thousand profit, the math usually does not work.
segments.visual: S corp cost benefit analysis chart

segments.voice: Retirement accounts that reduce SE tax base. Solo 401(k) contributions made as employer profit sharing reduce the net earnings calculation for SE tax purposes. The two thousand twenty-six limits per IRS Notice 2025-67 are seventy thousand for the combined employee plus employer contributions. SEP-IRA contributions also reduce SE tax base when structured correctly.

UK readers, the National Insurance equivalent. Self-employed Class 2 contributions were merged into Class 4 starting from twenty twenty-four. Class 4 is six percent on profits between twelve thousand five hundred seventy and fifty thousand two hundred seventy pounds, then two percent above that. Plus regular income tax on top. HMRC's online Self Assessment portal calculates everything together once you enter the figures.
segments.visual: UK HMRC self assessment portal screen

segments.voice: The voluntary contributions trap and benefit. Class 2 NI used to be how self-employed people earned credits toward the State Pension. After the merger, low-earning self-employed who fall below the small profits threshold can pay voluntary Class 2 to keep building State Pension entitlement. At three pounds forty-five a week, it is dramatically cheaper than Class 3 voluntary contributions and worth knowing if you have lean years.

Quarterly pre-pay strategy. Most freelancers wait until each quarterly deadline to fund the payment. A better move is treating the tax bucket as money that does not exist. Transfer the percentage immediately on every payment received. Treat the tax account like the IRS already owns it, because functionally, they do. This single behavioral shift fixes the cash flow surprise that catches new freelancers in year one.

The real underrated move, hiring your kids if you have them. If your kid is under eighteen and you operate as a sole prop or single-member LLC, wages paid to them are not subject to FICA per IRC section 3121(b)(3). The first fifteen thousand for two thousand twenty-six matches the standard deduction, so the kid pays zero income tax too. Just need real work and real records.

What real work qualifies for kids. Filing, basic bookkeeping, social media content creation, photography for the business, packing orders if you sell physical products. The IRS rejects fake jobs immediately, so keep timesheets, written job descriptions, and a clear pay rate that matches market rates for the actual work. Done right, this strategy moves taxable income to a zero-bracket family member legally.
segments.visual: teen helping parent at home office

segments.voice: QBI deduction is still in play, even with SE tax. The qualified business income deduction under IRC section 199A reduces income tax but does not reduce SE tax. So a freelancer at fifty-five thousand profit gets the QBI deduction stacked on top of SE tax obligations. Keep them separate in your mental model, otherwise the math gets confusing fast.

When to actually pay a CPA. If your profit is under thirty thousand, free filing through the IRS Free File program covers most of what you need. Above that, a CPA who specializes in freelancers usually saves two to five times their fee through deduction optimization and entity structure. The National Association of Tax Professionals directory lets you search by specialty and zip code.

The audit reality for freelancers. Per the IRS Data Book two thousand twenty-three, Schedule C filers earning under one hundred thousand had an audit rate of about zero point five percent. Above two hundred thousand, the rate climbs to about one point seven percent. The biggest red flag is reporting net losses three years running, which the IRS uses to challenge whether the activity is a legitimate business or a hobby per Section 183.

Last big move, contribute to a Roth IRA in low-income years. If your taxable income is low because you are reinvesting in your business or you had a slow year, a Roth IRA contribution lets you lock in tax-free growth at a low rate. The two thousand twenty-six limit is seven thousand, eight thousand if you are over fifty. The income phase-out starts at one hundred fifty thousand single.
segments.visual: roth IRA contribution low income year

segments.voice: Subscribe to FinanzasClara and hit the bell. What's coming next week, a deep dive on the S corp salary calculation that an IRS auditor walked one Reddit user through, including the exact reasonable compensation worksheet they used. Drop a comment with your toughest self-employment tax question and I will tackle the top three in a follow-up.
